content.assessment: Capping only the age-10 entry point retains the geographically concentrated 8-year and 4-year tracks, so the network-level distributional problem (elite provision clustered in Budapest and a few centers, with only one consistent rural top-10 performer) persists largely unchanged. This partial approach may shift capacity pressure to the age-12 entry point without resolving the underlying geographic access disparity, and the network lens offers no strong evidence either supporting or undermining feasibility beyond noting the funnel is only partially closed.
opportunities: Could serve as a testbed for assessing network-level effects of entry-point elimination before considering broader structural change
strengths: Reduces the number of selection stages requiring geographic coordination from three to two, marginally simplifying network planning
threats: Displaced demand concentrating at the age-12 entry point could intensify capacity pressure at that stage without any corresponding network expansion
Segregation mechanisms tied to school-level disadvantage concentration may persist unchanged since retained tracks share similar geographic and capacity profiles
weaknesses: Retains the 8-year and 4-year tracks that are themselves geographically concentrated in Budapest and a few regional centers, so network-level access inequity is not resolved
Does not address rural teacher shortages or the shrinking primary feeder network, since surviving tracks still depend on the same constrained rural capacity
